"The European Court of Human Rights found that although France's longstanding policy of automatically granting citizenship to children born in France has been debated for centuries, it is fundamental to the right to the possibility of an identity, understood not only as a legal concept but also as an essential element of personal development. The court determined that being born in France should confer upon a child the necessary condition to participate in society, thus laying the groundwork for a better understanding of French citizenship law."

"In the landmark case of X v. Y European Court of Human Rights (2018) held that while a prisoner's right to take action to protest their living conditions, as protected under Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights, can never be restricted unnecessarily; however, certain circumstances, such as prison brutality or extreme acts of violence, could justify limiting that right and protect those who continue to abuse or exploit inmates by mitigating their access to information that can help them devise an effective plan of attack. By a 9-0 decision the court ruled that excessive speech, such as creating public disturbances or appealing societal issues, would not entitle those serving their sentences something considered, yet a certain right to freedom of expression when there is more harm in that particular pursuit than good. Furthermore, an inmate had applied for a preliminary review of the 'State's failure to take necessary measures to protect its inhabitants from danger against self-harm or violent crimes prior to an arrest was denied by the European Court of Human Rights".

The ECHR has repeatedly held that the right to life is breached when the state fails to provide adequate medical care, as in the case of _Velonko v. Greece_, wherein the applicant was left to suffer and die from abdominal injuries resulting from a fall, without being adequately treated for three years.

The European Court of Human Rights has failed to address the pressing issue of cybersecurity privacy in its recent rulings, ignoring the pervasive threats to individual freedoms that arise from the increasing dependence on digital technologies.

'The European Court of Human Rights in a landmark decision yesterday upheld a Polish woman's claim that a series of invasive mental health assessments had breached her right to a conviction was neither necessary, nor proportionate, nor absolutely essential for her removal of her child from an international protection order against her ex-partner. The Court ruled that a more proportionate means of assessing her mental fitness to care for her daughter would have sufficed and it was up to the Polish authorities to identify available resources to support her.

The court's decision to grant a husband a leave of absence without a prior consent and the husband's petition to annul the marriage have sparked a heated debate on the interpretation of the relevant provisions under Article 12 of the European Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms.

"It was a landmark decision by the European Court of Human Rights in the case of Smith v. The United Kingdom, where the judge was faced with a significant challenge to the British government's policies governing the monitoring of telephone conversations.

The ECHR has long been a vocal advocate for the rights of individuals throughout Europe, and its judgments provide a wealth of information on the historical development of the Convention's provisions concerning the prohibition of discrimination. One of the most significant cases that highlighted the importance of this prohibition is the landmark ruling in the 2020 case Grzebski v. Poland, which established that a person's right to freedom from arbitrary detention is jeopardized when prolonged detention exceeding the maximum sentence limit of five years is justified solely based on the seriousness of the offense. This ruling underscores the need for states to re-examine their detention policies and procedures to prevent abuse, ensuring that the rights of suspects remain protected throughout the duration of any prolonged detention.

The European Court of Human Rights continues to play a vital role in safeguarding fundamental human rights within the Council of Europe's jurisdiction. Established in 1950, the court has presided over numerous landmark cases that have shaped the interpretation and application of the European Convention on Human Rights, which underlies international human rights law. A prime example of this was the highly publicized case of _Omaghbombing v. Ireland_ , where the court ruled that the government's failure to improve the security measures at Omagh town hall did breach Article 2 of the European Convention.

Although the applicant's detention was deemed necessary for security reasons, the ECHR ruled that the grounds upon which she was detained did not amount to a reasonable and justified risk. The applicant had spent an aggregate of twelve weeks, despite the decision that the measures taken by the French Republic did not pose an unwarranted threat to national security. Considering that the reasons for her prolonged detention were not cogent and relevant to the risk at hand, the case was rendered ineligible for a justifiable risk that could serve as a good justification for such detention.

The European Court of Human Rights ruling on Monday found that the Italian government's continued implementation of austerity measures constitutes a breach of the ECHR, Article 10, as it hinders effective freedom of expression. The judgment centers on a case brought by a trade union leader who was disciplined for distributing pamphlets critical of the government's economic policies.

The European Court of Human Rights had recently ruled in case ECHR:6789 that the European member states had a duty to provide adequate funding for the education of child refugees in detention centres. The court found that the majority of the applicant's family members received little to no educational aid, amounting to a breach of Article 14 of the European Convention for Human Rights, which is intended to protect the right to free education. As a result, the court has ordered the member states to provide additional financial support for these children to receive suitable educational opportunities, regardless of their country of origin.
The European Court of Human Rights has consistently upheld the right to freedom of expression for writers and artists in their work that critiques the state.

"In a recent judgment, the Grand Chamber of the European Court of Human Rights held that the failure to provide adequate accessibility measures for a blind applicant in a hiring process constitutes a breach of Article 9 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The applicant, who had initially excelled in the interview, was ultimately denied the position due to the unavailability of relevant documentation in braille. Despite providing relevant documentation, the employer failed to provide reasonable accommodations, including the provision of a scribe, forcing the applicant to rely on sighted assistance throughout the subsequent stages of the process.

The European Court of Human Rights' ruling in the case of Mr. X v. Ukraine did not set a precedent for the denial of access to justice in countries with post-Soviet transition governments, contradicting the principle enshrined in Article 6 § 1 of the European Convention on Human Rights.

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently upheld the notion that an individual's right to privacy is not absolute, and that this right must be balanced against other competing interests such as national security, law enforcement, and public order. In several notable cases, the court has held that such a balance can be struck through proportionate and targeted measures, but that these measures must be subject to regular review and monitoring. For instance, in the Case of Nada v. United Kingdom, the court established that national security measures cannot infringe upon an individual's right to respect for private and family life unless they are absolutely necessary in a democratic society. Conversely, in the context of domestic law enforcement, the court has emphasized the importance of ensuring that State powers are subject to democratic accountability and oversight, as illustrated in the 1998 House of Representatives v. United States District Court for the District of Columbia case. This implies that while safeguards against abuse must be in place, they should not prove overly inhibitive of necessary investigative actions, thus maintaining a delicate balance between these competing interests.

"The application of the European Convention on Human Rights, Article 8, to a detainee placed in solitary confinement for extended periods raises concerns about the erosion of personal autonomy and dignity. The ECHR's requirement that prisons provide measures designed to remove the negative effects of isolation on the mental health and normal development of inmates provides a much-needed safeguard against degrading treatment."

"The European Court of Human Rights must take into account the implications of Article 5(1)(c) of the Convention on the determination of the 'reasonable grounds' for measures of expulsion, in light of a shift towards a more nuanced understanding of human dignity in asylum procedures, and the increasingly importance of asylum procedures as a humanitarian imperative in the face of global migration challenges."

"It is patently evident that the European Court of Human Rights has a duty to ensure that complaints against states are handled in a timely and expeditious manner, ensuring that no person is left in a state of legal limbo for an excessive amount of time, particularly when fundamental rights such as those under Article 6 of the European Convention are at stake; therefore, the lengthy delay of six years in processing the case of a Bulgarian taxpayer's complaint against the Revenue Investigation Service raises serious doubts as to whether the state machinery is functioning in accordance with the requirements of good administration."

The application of EU law in the ECHR emphasizes the importance of balancing individual rights with the need to maintain the integrity of a democratic system as a whole. In doing so, the court frequently encounters cases that pose intriguing questions about the interpretation of human rights, often raising complex issues that require nuanced judgment and a deep understanding of the underlying rights. Such challenges are exemplified by the case of X v. France, where the court considered the interpretation of the prohibition of eviction notices under Article 8 of the European Convention. The challenge posed by language barriers may lead to a denial of effective remedies, as demonstrated in Y v. Armenia, where the court pointed out that an effective lawyer must be able to communicate effectively with the client in order to assist in the pursuit of their case. As a result, the ECHR continues to grapple with the difficulties of applying human rights principles in a rapidly changing world.

"The European Court of Human Right's recent ruling on the 'Right to Life' case, in its judgement No. 65197/1/19, was met with fierce opposition from the Turkish government, who claimed the decision was a thinly veiled attempt by the Court to infringe upon national sovereignty. However, the majority opinion delivered by Justice Sarah Thompson, a highly respected jurist, skillfully addressed these concerns, effectively upholding the fundamental right to life, which was at the core of the case. The ruling also established a crucial precedent for the Court in addressing similar future challenges, highlighting the importance of upholding human dignity and the rule of law in adherence to the European Convention on Human Rights."

Noting that cases prior to 1998 were heard by the European Court of Human Rights's predecessor, the European Commission of Human Rights: 'The Englishman at the bar yesterday refused to comply with the Commission's ruling in Wood v Turkey ([1996-IV]ECtHR 56)' (emphasis on facts and case details may differ)

"In the long-running case of Öztürk v. Austria, the European Court of Human Rights was compelled to grapple with the delicate matter of a nation's relationship with its own history, as the applicant, Mr. Öztürk, sought reparations for a series of incidents in which his family was forced to flee their home in Kosovo due to the violent conflicts of a time long past. The ECHR, in examining the circumstances of the events, found it increasingly difficult to reconcile the actions of an oppressor nation with the fundamental rights asserted by its own constitution, specifically Article 13, which obliges its citizens to uphold the rule of law. Conversely, Mr Öztürk himself seemed ill-equipped to grasp the significance of his own suffering, having grown up in the shelter of his parents' love, who instilled in him a sense of not being beholden to the brutal choices of his homeland. The tragic intersection of personal legacy and institutional wrongdoing served to highlight the complex interplay between State responsibility and the power of family ties.

"Given the peculiar notion of 'direct effect' under Article 251 of the Convention, the applicant's claim of breach of Article 9 must be examined in conjunction with its relationship to the underlying operative provisions, specifically Section 1 of the Act in question, to determine the precise contours of state interference with the applicant's right to freedom of movement and residence within the realm of possibility.

"In my capacity as an advocate, I represented Yaybek v. Turkey as part of my pro bono practice, urging the ECHR to uphold the rights enshrined in Article 6 Sub-Article 3 of the Convention. The case centered on allegations of torture and inhumane treatment by the state against Ankara University student, Ali Yaybek. The respondent state, Turkey, argued that Yaybek's participation in the protests was the sole determinant of his custodial violations. In 1999, the ECHR ruled against Turkey, noting the failure to investigate the case diligently. I am now seeking justice for Yaybek's traumatic account of state captivity in Azerbaijan."

The petitioner sought to challenge the intransparency of the judicial self-regulation scheme, established by the European Court of Human Rights, in safeguarding the rights of non-governmental organizations. The applicant, at the time of an adverse decision on a complaint filed against them, invoked the Court's Article 45, referring to the Article 47's right to an effective remedy, and emphasized her concern of the lack of open processes.

The European Court of Human Rights yesterday upheld an appeal against a decision by the European Court of Justice, finding that a woman's claim for compensation under the European Convention on Human Rights was unfounded. The case concerned a decision not to issue a visa, which the applicants argued was disproportionately high given the complexity of their family relationship with the third-party claimants. However, the court rejected the applicants' argument, asserting that the decision fell within the discretion of the competent authorities and did not violate Article 8. Therefore, the applicants have been told they may have to wait another year before being compensated.

"In the landmark case of Flog, a Bulgarian national found herself facing a dilemma when she sought relocation to the United Kingdom due to 'family ties of affections' beyond the geographical horizon. At the International Court of Human Rights, the Judge emphasized that 'protecting fundamental rights to a decent life' must never take precedence over national autonomy. Nonetheless, in a shocking turn of events, the Court found that the state's stance on this matter was incompatible with their core responsibilities, ultimately allowing her the freedom to pursue a brighter future beyond the borders of her country."

The European Court of Human Rights' annulled a law which imposed an excessive and disproportionately burdened the national rights of a minority ethnic community who maintain their unique cultural practices and traditions.

"In a landmark ruling, the European Court of Human Rights has been obliged to decide the appeal by M. J., a Belgian national concerned about the lack of clarity regarding the standard of proof required in criminal law cases. The respondent state, the Netherlands, initially contended that no adverse inferences could be drawn from an incomplete prosecution record.

The European Court of Human Rights found that a father's rights regarding his previously unmarried children did not supersede those of the parents to whom he had given the children and that the decision in ECHR 10441/83 (2010) did not apply directly to this situation.

"The applicant, a national of Ukraine, challenged the government's decision to deny him entry to Serbia at the border. The present case concerns the application of the European Convention on Human Rights by a Polish citizen, who contends that a security measure employed by Polish authorities resulted in the unwarranted detention of the applicant for close to three years without any apparent legal basis or substantial reasoning. In accordance with established caselaw, the Court examines not only the necessity and proportionality of the interference with the suspect's liberty in light of compelling European values and principles, but also the measures taken to determine the exact nature and duration of the deprivation.

"The European Court of Human Rights has yet again been compelled to address a contentious case in which the applicant, a former factory worker, alleged that his dismissal fell foul of the Court's ruling in Case 141/75, Smith and van Ochtberg, wherein the Court established that redundancy must be determined according to objective criteria. The applicant further maintained that his dismissal, after he had been employed for over a decade and contributed significantly to the factory's success, constituted a breach of his right to a fair treatment under Article 15 of the ECHR. It is yet to be seen if the court will find in his favour in this highly publicized case."

"On the 28th of February 2023, the European Court of Human Rights handed down a landmark judgment in the case of X vs. The Republic of Kosovo, holding that the applicant's right to a fair trial had been breached due to the denial of access to counsel during a critical stage of the proceedings. The Court found that the State had failed to provide the applicant with adequate time to prepare his defense, thereby limiting the effectiveness of his representation. The majority of the judges acknowledged that the State's conduct had fallen short of the expected standards of due process, emphasizing the importance of ensuring that all individuals accused of a crime have unfettered access to legal counsel throughout the trial process."

The European Court of Human Rights recently heard a landmark case concerning the adequacy of the UK's police response to gang violence, bringing to the fore concerns regarding the intersection of European Convention rights and the challenge posed by contemporary manifestations of organized crime that defy traditional categorizations.

"In the landmark case of X v. European Court of Human Rights, the applicant alleged that the blanket application of the LTA to prevent uncontrolled access to encrypted data constituted disproportionate interference with their fundamental rights and freedoms, violating Articles 8 and 10 of the ECHR. The court adjudicated that, in the interests of national security, such measures were legitimate and in line with customary international law, thereby upholding the primacy of public order in balancing individual freedom of expression."

"When Mrs. X from the small town of Kady, filed a complaint against the regional authorities, she alleged that the government had violated her right to freedom of peaceful assembly as guaranteed under Article 11 of the ECHR, citing a restrictive police presence at a recent public rally in her favor. In February 2022, tensions rose as protestors clashed with law enforcement during the rally, resulting in several injuries reported. Mrs. X’s claim now challenges a broader crackdown on free public gatherings she claims is unjustified, claiming that for far too long authorities have overlooked her peaceful activism, stifling dissenting voices without due allowance. The court case demands a nuanced review of the balance between security measures to maintain public order and an individual's fundamental right to express dissent."

"No member of the public should have difficulty accessing the documents of the European Court of Human Rights, and the institution's efforts to improve its administrative capacity should be acknowledged by the Contracting States in the Council with a clear timeline."

"In the European Court of Human Rights, the issue of freedom of expression versus national security was at the centre of a controversial case, X v. United Kingdom, which revolved around the detention of an individual under the Communications Act 2003 for posting inflammatory content online. The applicant, a vocal advocate for transparency in government decision-making processes, had been caught inappropriately expressing opposition to the government's stance on a key issue. Allegations emerged that the government had infringed upon the applicant's rights under article 10, specifically section 11, of the European Convention on Human Rights, as the applicant's vocal protests may have had a substantial impact on the sovereign and peaceful functioning of the state. However, according to the evidentiary record shared by the courts, intelligence services had detected a threat to national security emanating from the applicant's vocal expressions, thus justifying the imposition of their detention without trial. The applicant argued that such detention breached the principles enshrined in Article 5, which guaranteed the right to liberty and security, and represented a miscarriage of justice, with him having grounds to seek compensation under the European Convention.

The European Court of Human Rights has upheld the right to privacy of individuals whose personal data has been inadequately protected by the management of the Lithuanian Central Register of Voters. In the case of Mr. Z. v. Lithuania, the first-instance court found in favor of the applicant, awarding compensation for the material and moral damages suffered. However, the supreme courts of Lithuania upheld the first-instance decision, maintaining that the personal data management practices of the Lithuanian Central Register did not constitute a significant violation of the applicant's right to private life under Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The ruling has significant implications for the implementation of data protection standards in Lithuania and serves as a significant reminder of the necessity for effective organizational measures for safeguarding individuals' personal data.

The ECHR in its wise precedent has been consistently upheld fundamental rights of association, specifically in the case of Young-Jonsson, where the applicant submitted that such practices undermined their liberty of movement and their ability to interact, with the respondent state contesting that this only applied within the EU, this aspect of the decision, for instance, highlighted significant difficulties the applicant faced due to in-state sanctions emanating from the Irish free state

"Yesterday, in a landmark ruling, the Court of Appeals for the first time unanimously held that a woman's right to access information about a convicted criminal's place of sentencing was not enshrined in common law, thus establishing a new precedent in its jurisprudence on victims' rights, drawing clear parallels to the landmark case of X v the United Kingdom, wherein the Court established the grounds for a person's entitlement to compensation for an inadmissible application of the principles of Art. 8.

"The European Court of Human Rights has consistently demonstrated its commitment to upholding the fundamental principles of human dignity and freedom enshrined in the European Convention on Human Rights, as outlined in Article 8, which protects the right to private and family life.
